description.en: Operate GenAI across Foundry, Bedrock, and Vertex through one gateway: routing, token budgets, guardrails, evaluation, and governance — the capstone pattern that ties the whole track together.

story_variants.en:
## Eleven Teams, Three Platforms, Zero Control

Eighteen months into the GenAI era, an enterprise audit finds: eleven teams calling models directly — four on Azure AI Foundry, three on Amazon Bedrock, two on Vertex AI, two on external APIs nobody procured. Each has its own API keys, its own prompt logs (or none), its own idea of PII redaction, and its own invoice surprise. No one can answer the board's three questions: *What are we spending? What data is leaving? Are the answers any good?*

The cure has become the consensus architecture of 2025-2026: the **GenAI gateway** — one governed front door between every application and every model, on every cloud. It is the AI-era descendant of the API gateway, and it is where this entire track converges.

## What the Gateway Does

A production GenAI gateway (whether built on an LLM-aware proxy, delivered by API-management platforms — Azure API Management's GenAI gateway capabilities, cloud-native and third-party LLM gateways — or composed in-house) owns six concerns:

- **Unified API & credential custody.** Apps speak one interface; the gateway holds per-platform credentials (via workload identity, of course — secrets lesson) and translates to Foundry, Bedrock, or Vertex behind the scenes. Model swaps become config changes.
- **Routing & failover.** Requests routed by policy — capability tier, cost, latency, region, quota headroom — with fallback chains when a provider throttles or a region degrades (your DR thinking, applied per-request).
- **Cost control.** Token metering per team/app/user, budgets with enforcement (soft-warn, hard-block), caching of repeated prompts, and batch scheduling for tolerant workloads. This is FinOps' unit-economics discipline applied to tokens — cost per conversation, per resolved ticket, per document processed.
- **Safety & data guardrails.** Centralized PII redaction, prompt-injection screening, content filtering (in addition to platform-native filters like Bedrock Guardrails or Foundry content filters), and *residency-aware routing* — EU-origin requests pinned to EU-region endpoints, closing the loop with your data-gravity lesson.
- **Observability.** Every request traced (OTel semantic conventions for GenAI exist and are maturing), with tokens, latency, model version, and outcome — feeding the same unified observability plane as everything else.
- **Audit & governance.** Who asked what, of which model, with what data classification — the evidence trail that the EU AI Act era (its GPAI obligations began applying in 2025) and your internal AI-governance policies increasingly demand.

## LLMOps Around the Gateway

The gateway is the enforcement point; **LLMOps** is the lifecycle around it:

| Lifecycle concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prompt/flow tooling | Foundry prompt flow & evaluations | Bedrock Prompt Management | Vertex AI Studio / prompt tooling |
| Evaluation | Foundry evaluators (groundedness, safety) | Bedrock model evaluation / LLM-as-judge | Vertex Gen AI evaluation service |
| Guardrails | Content filters + Prompt Shields | Bedrock Guardrails | Safety filters / Model Armor |
| Registry & versioning | Foundry model catalog + registries | Bedrock + SageMaker registry | Vertex Model Registry |
| Agent orchestration | Foundry Agent Service | Bedrock AgentCore | Vertex Agent Builder / ADK |

Operating disciplines that separate production from demo:

- **Version everything:** prompts, model versions, retrieval indexes, guardrail configs. A quality regression must be bisectable to *which* change — and provider-side model updates mean pinned versions plus scheduled re-evaluation, because your dependency changes underneath you even when you change nothing.
- **Evaluate continuously:** golden datasets and LLM-as-judge scoring on every prompt/model change, plus sampled production scoring for drift. Without eval automation, every model upgrade is a leap of faith performed in production.
- **Canary models like code:** new model versions get shadow traffic or small percentages first. The gateway makes this trivial — it owns routing.

> **Callout:** The gateway inverts the political problem, too. Instead of begging eleven teams to adopt logging, redaction, and budgets, you offer one endpoint where all of it is already done — and make direct model access an exception requiring justification. Carrot first, policy second.

## Real Failure Modes

- **The gateway that became a bottleneck.** One under-provisioned proxy in one region serving three clouds' traffic: every AI feature in the company now shares a single point of failure and a latency tax. Gateways must be deployed per-region/per-cloud close to callers, stateless and horizontally scaled, sharing *policy* rather than *infrastructure*.
- **Shadow AI regrowth.** The gateway adds friction (slow onboarding, stingy quotas); teams quietly return to direct API keys. Adoption is a product problem: onboarding in minutes, generous defaults, self-service dashboards.
- **Budget alerts without teeth.** Token budgets that only email warnings; a runaway agent loop burns a quarter's allocation in a weekend. Hard limits with per-principal circuit breakers are the guardrail that matters at 3 a.m. — agentic workloads especially, where one bug means ten thousand model calls, not one.
- **The eval-less migration.** Provider announces model deprecation; the gateway swaps versions in an afternoon — capability regressions surface as customer complaints because no golden-dataset gate stood between config change and production. Routing flexibility without evaluation rigor just lets you break things faster.

## The Capstone Thread

Trace one request and you will see the whole track: an EU user's query hits the gateway (zero-trust: identity + device verified), is routed to an EU-region model endpoint (residency), enriched by retrieval-at-source RAG (hybrid patterns) over a permission-filtered index, metered per-token against the team budget (FinOps), traced end-to-end (observability), with a failover chain if the primary platform throttles (DR) — all through credentials no human has ever seen (secrets, identity federation), inside landing zones that made every one of those controls enforceable (strategy module). That is cross-cloud architecture: not a diagram, but a request that survives contact with production.

In the final challenge you will build the gateway's core: a policy router with token-cost accounting — the exact loop running inside every LLM gateway in production today.

description.en: One governed front door between every application and every model platform — routing, metering, guardrails, and audit live in the gateway, not in eleven codebases.

left.label: Eleven teams, direct API keys, per-team logging and redaction (or none)

right.label: One gateway: unified API, credential custody, budgets, guardrails, traces

description.en: Direct model access multiplies every governance obligation by team count. The gateway implements each control exactly once.

explanation.en: Relative cost units per model tier — the gateway meters every request against them.
Long-context or high-complexity requests earn the frontier model; everything else is cheaper by policy, not by hope.
Medium complexity routes to the balanced tier — most enterprise traffic lands here.
The default is the cheapest tier: cost discipline as the fall-through, not the exception.

description.en: The per-request pipeline inside a production GenAI gateway — every stage maps to a lesson in this track.
Deterministic routing with cost accounting: the loop at the heart of every LLM gateway in production.

instruction.en: Build the GenAI gateway's core: policy routing plus token-cost accounting. Implement route(req) -> str using rules IN ORDER: if req['tokens'] > 4000 or req['complexity'] == 'high' return 'frontier'; elif req['complexity'] == 'medium' return 'balanced'; else return 'fast-small'. Implement cost_units(req, model) -> int as (req['tokens'] // 1000) * COST_PER_1K[model]. For each request in REQUESTS (in order) print exactly '<id> -> <model> (cost=<cost>)'. Then print 'Total cost units: <total>'. This routing-plus-metering loop is what turns ungoverned model sprawl into an operated platform.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
r1 -> fast-small (cost=1)
r2 -> balanced (cost=6)
r3 -> frontier (cost=120)
r4 -> frontier (cost=15)
Total cost units: 142

en: route checks the frontier condition first — r3 goes to frontier because of token count even though complexity is low.
cost_units uses integer division: (req['tokens'] // 1000) * COST_PER_1K[model], so 8000 tokens on frontier = 8 * 15 = 120.
Accumulate the total while looping and finish with f'Total cost units: {total}' — 1 + 6 + 120 + 15 = 142.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert route({"id": "a", "tokens": 1000, "complexity": "low"}) == "fast-small"
assert route({"id": "b", "tokens": 2000, "complexity": "medium"}) == "balanced"
assert route({"id": "c", "tokens": 8000, "complexity": "low"}) == "frontier"
assert route({"id": "d", "tokens": 1000, "complexity": "high"}) == "frontier"
assert cost_units({"id": "e", "tokens": 8000}, "frontier") == 120
assert cost_units({"id": "f", "tokens": 2000}, "balanced") == 6
assert cost_units({"id": "g", "tokens": 1000}, "fast-small") == 1

q.en: What core problem does the GenAI gateway pattern solve?

en: It replaces the need for evaluation
It puts one governed enforcement point — credentials, routing, budgets, guardrails, audit — between all apps and all model platforms

q.en: Why must token budgets have hard enforcement rather than warning emails only?

en: Agentic workloads can loop: one bug means ten thousand model calls before any human reads a warning
Soft limits are deprecated in all SDKs

q.en: The gateway makes model swaps a config change. What discipline must gate that flexibility?

en: Golden-dataset evaluation before routing changes reach production — otherwise you just break things faster

q.en: How does the gateway close the loop with data-residency obligations?

en: By encrypting all prompts with a single global key
It cannot — residency is a storage-only concern
Residency-aware routing: pinning requests from a jurisdiction to model endpoints in permitted regions
